symptom: Outdoor unit hums for several seconds at call for cooling and then trips on overload. Compressor does not spin up; fan may or may not start depending on whether the run capacitor is dual-section.
diagnosis: Failed run capacitor producing locked-rotor condition. The compressor draws stalled-rotor current because the start phase never establishes a rotating field; capacitor microfarad reading is below the rated value on the nameplate.
procedure: Kill power at the disconnect and verify de-energized at the contactor with a meter.
Bleed the capacitor across both terminals through a properly rated resistor — DO NOT short with a screwdriver.
Read the labeled rated microfarads; measure actual capacitance with a meter that handles motor-run caps.
Replace with the same voltage rating and microfarad value; observe terminal labeling for dual-section caps (HERM / FAN / C).
Restart and confirm compressor and condenser fan both run; verify amperage is within nameplate.
safety_notes: Capacitors can hold a lethal charge even after power is removed; always bleed before touching terminals.
Wrong terminal landing on a dual-section cap will burn out the start winding within seconds.
Locked-rotor lasting more than the rated stall time can damage the compressor permanently — diagnose quickly.
